metadata.description: 创建型模式: Creation patterns abstract the instantiation process of objects, making the system independent of how objects are created. To provide a more concrete example, let's consider the Factory Method pattern. This pattern defines an interface for creating an object, but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate. Here is a brief Python code example: ```python from abc import ABC, abstractmethod # Define the Creator (Factory) Interface class Creator(ABC): @abstractmethod def factory_method(self): pass def some_operation(self) -> str: product = self.factory_method() return f"Creator: The same creator's code has just worked with {product.operation()}" # Concrete Creators implement the Factory Method to decide which product class to instantiate. class ConcreteCreator1(Creator): def factory_method(self) -> 'ProductA': return ProductA() class ConcreteCreator2(Creator): def factory_method(self) -> 'ProductB': return ProductB() # Define the Product Interface class Product(ABC): @abstractmethod def operation(self) -> str: pass # Concrete Products correspond to different subclasses of creators. class ProductA(Product): def operation(self) -> str: return "{Result of the ConcreteProductA}" class ProductB(Product): def operation(self) -> str: return "{Result of the ConcreteProductB}" def client_code(creator: Creator) -> None: print(f"Client: I'm not aware of the creator's class, but it still works.\n" f"{creator.some_operation()}", end="") if __name__ == "__main__": print("App: Launched with the ConcreteCreator1.") client_code(ConcreteCreator1()) print("\n") print("App: Launched with the ConcreteCreator2.") client_code(ConcreteCreator2()) ``` In this example, `Creator` is an abstract class that defines a factory method. The concrete creators (`ConcreteCreator1`, `ConcreteCreator2`) implement the factory method to return different product objects (`ProductA`, `ProductB`). This way, the system remains independent of how and where products are created.
